coredata.dc:title: Chemo-physical mechanisms of high-strength cement composites with suprastructure of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Win, Thwe Thwe
Prasittisopin, Lapyote
Nganglumpoon, Rungkiat
Pinthong, Piriya
Watmanee, Suthasinee
Tolek, Weerachon
Panpranot, Joongjai
coredata.dc:description: Recently, there has been considerable interest in utilizing various forms of graphene derivatives for producing high-strength concrete. Among these derivatives are superstructure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), particularly in their assemblies of carbon dots, which is innovative in cement. This research investigates the impact of graphene derivatives known as supra-GQDs on the mechanical properties and microstructure analysis of cement composites, compared with the control mixture and GQDs solution. The results found that supra-GQDs exhibit enhanced mechanical characteristics. The composite containing 1.2 % supra-GQDs had higher compressive and flexural strengths than the control by 40 % and 108 %, respectively. The study also identified a microstructural bridging mechanism involving the seeding and crystal growth of the C-S-H phase, leading to refined pore structure and less nano-, meso-, and micro-pores. The measured total pore volume reduced by 30 % when compared to GQDs solution. This investigation provides novel insight into the potential of utilizing supra-GQDs in cement composites, opening promising possibilities for high-performance concrete in the construction industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Supra-GQDs
Mechanical performance
Microstructure
Cement composites
Nanomaterial

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to the Second Century Fund (C2F) for the postdoctoral researcher fellowship at Chulalongkorn University . This project was also funded by National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and Chulalongkorn University ( N42A660629 ), and by Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fund Chulalongkorn University ( SOCF67250015 ). The authors thank CrystalLyte Co., Ltd., for providing the graphene quantum dots. The authors also thank the Centre of Excellence in Innovative Construction Materials of Chulalongkorn University for the lab equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sieve analysis of fine aggregate and ASTM limits.
Experimental scheme: (a) Flow test, (b) Compression test, and (c) Flexural test.
Characterization results of supra-GQDs: (a) SEM-EDX, (b) XRD, (c)Raman, and (d) TEM.
The effect of supra-GQDs on the initial setting time of cement composites system.
Mechanical properties of cement composites containing supra-GQDs: (a) Compressive strength; (b) % increase in compressive strength; (c) Flexural strength; and (d) % increase in flexural strength.
Physical properties of mortars containing BC: (a) SEM-EDX; (b) VPV; (c) Absorption; and (d) Density.
Comparative results of supra-GQDs vs. GQDs solution in cement.
Chemical and physical properties of OPC.
Experimental details of supra-GQDs cement composites.

row.entry: Shape and dimension of the sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Elemental compositions and impurities of supra-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Chemo-physical mechanisms of high-strength cement composites with suprastructure of graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Architectural Technology Research Unit, Department of Architecture, Faculty of Architecture, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, there has been considerable interest in utilizing various forms of graphene derivatives for producing high-strength concrete. Among these derivatives are superstructure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), particularly in their assemblies of carbon dots, which is innovative in cement. This research investigates the impact of graphene derivatives known as supra-GQDs on the mechanical properties and microstructure analysis of cement composites, compared with the control mixture and GQDs solution. The results found that supra-GQDs exhibit enhanced mechanical characteristics. The composite containing 1.2 % supra-GQDs had higher compressive and flexural strengths than the control by 40 % and 108 %, respectively. The study also identified a microstructural bridging mechanism involving the seeding and crystal growth of the C-S-H phase, leading to refined pore structure and less nano-, meso-, and micro-pores. The measured total pore volume reduced by 30 % when compared to GQDs solution. This investigation provides novel insight into the potential of utilizing supra-GQDs in cement composites, opening promising possibilities for high-performance concrete in the construction industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Concrete is the main material of contemporary infrastructure, from skyscrapers to bridges and dams. High-strength concrete (HSC) can be used as modern building materials by improving mechanical properties, durability, and sustainability. Urbanization and population development have increased concrete demand. Taller buildings, wide-span bridges, and seismically and environmentally resistant construction are needed. Typically, conventional concrete, with a compressive strength ranging from 20 to 40 MPa, has proven insufficient in meeting the stringent demands of these contemporary applications. As such, our focus turns to high-strength concrete (HSC), a material capable of exceeding these conventional limitations. HSC, characterized by compressive strengths surpassing 55 MPa and often exceeding 140 MPa, holds significant promise for addressing the challenges posed by modern construction needs (Shah et al., 2019). The importance of maintaining building and infrastructure structural integrity has become increasingly significant due to urbanization, climate change, and natural disasters. Traditional concrete possesses significant strength, but it may not adequately satisfy the requirements of cost-effectiveness, or environmental sustainability. Contemporary architectural design places high regard on structures characterized by escalating verticality and expansive horizontal spans. These projects have the potential to be utilized by the HSC.

Despite extensive research on HSC, the primary emphasis has been on enhancing performance and reducing cement content in order to promote sustainability. Advanced mineral and chemical additions were employed to address those issues (Win, 2022). Additives for cement materials—microfiber, graphene, and carbon dots (CDs)—are added. The utilization of nanoparticles in concrete has been observed to contribute to the enhancement of properties of concrete such as increased mechanical strength and durability (Win et al., 2023; Rashad, 2013; Rashad, 2013; Rashad, 2014). Nanotechnology can improve concrete's mechanical characteristics, durability, and sustainability while reducing its environmental impact (Wang et al., 2022). The advancement towards environmentally sustainable and technologically advanced buildings is accompanied by the potential utilization of HSC and nanomaterials, which have the capability to enhance the structural integrity and ecological compatibility of constructions.

Currently, graphene oxide (GO), one type of derivatives of graphene, is characterized by its two-dimensional structure and improved mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties (Khoei and Khorrami, 2016). The distinctive characteristics of GO have generated significant interest in exploring its capacity to enhance conventional concrete, which might potentially bring about a fundamental change in the building sector. The incorporation of GO into concrete mixtures has been reported to have several potential benefits, including the enhancement of compressive strength, reduction of permeability, improvement of crack resistance, and potential mitigation of the carbon footprint associated with concrete manufacturing (Mohammed et al., 2015; Horszczaruk, 2015). Nevertheless, it is crucial to understand the drawbacks linked to the utilization of GOas a substance added to concrete. Primarily, the financial aspect of GO poses an important challenge to its extensive adoption. The process of synthesizing GOof superior quality requires significant allocation of resources, resulting in a related market price that accurately represents these resource-intensive efforts. Furthermore, the dispersion of GO and its compatibility with cementitious materials can present significant challenges. The hydrophilic characteristic of the substance can result in agglomeration challenges when reacted with water, thereby compromising the qualities of the final concrete product (Kaur and Kothiyal, 2019; Liu, 2018). Consequently, achieving a uniform distribution of GO in the concrete matrix requires precise techniques and careful consideration.

To optimizing the mechanical properties and durability of concrete, researchers have explored various nanomaterials as potential additives (Ahmed et al., 2023; Piro et al., 2021; Piro et al., 2023). Salih et al. investigated that the incorporation of nano-calcium carbonate (nano-CaCO3) in cement paste significantly increased shear stress limit and yield stress. TGA tests revealed a 76 % reduction in weight loss at 800 °C with 1 % nano-CaCO3, showcasing its beneficial impact on cement properties (Salih, 2020). Ahmed et al. highlighted the advantageous impact of nanoclay (NC) in cement paste, notably increasing ultimate shear strength and yield stress (Mohammed, 2021). Moreover, they found that the best NC content was below 2 %. This lower NC percentage makes the properties of cement paste much better by improving the microstructure and reducing the number of large pores. This has a positive effect on both the flow properties and the compressive strength (Mohammed, 2021). Emad noticed that the addition of polymers substantially reduces the w/c by 12.5 % to 46 %, depending on polymer type and percentage. This reduction enhances the flow of the cement slurry by 7 % to 26 % (Emad et al., 2022). Furthermore, Qu (2022) investigated the potential of carbon dots (CDs) as a cost-effective alternative nanomaterial to enhance cement-based composites. Utilizing an improved microwave pyrolysis method, CDs significantly improve compressive strength by 16.8 % at 0.08 wt%. In addition, Song et al. (2023) presented the addition of carbon quantum dots (RHBCDs) derived from rice husk waste to vegetable protein-based surfactant in foamed concrete improves density, viscosity, and stability. Notably, with a 9.0 wt% addition of RHBCDs, foam displacement decreases, and after standard curing, it observed increased mechanical properties and reduced pore size distribution in the foamed concrete. Long et al. (2023) reported using designed CDs to enhance the anti-chloride-corrosion capability of reinforced concrete structures. The incorporation of 0.2 wt% CDs in cement paste significantly increases chloride binding capacity by 109 %; attributed to the promotion of calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) gels and Friedel’s salt (Fs) formation.

Among these, CDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with dimension smaller than 10–20 nm and high fluorescence (Liu et al., 2020). Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are CDs with 2D structure of a single or few graphene layers. CDs have gained significance due to their enhanced mechanical strength, high chemical stability, prolonged durability, resistance to corrosion, low cost, non-toxicity, and the capacity to change their luminous qualities. CDs in their general form have often demonstrated superior performance when compared to GO (Qu, 2022; Li, 2021; Sharma, 2019). However, using CDs in cement still has challenges regarding cement hydration and subsequent concrete hardening. The interaction between water and cementitious materials during the formation of the concrete matrix can lead to a reduction in the structural integrity of CDs, hence potentially lessening their positive influence on the characteristics of concrete (Qu, 2022).

Supra-CDs, known as “Supramolecular Carbon Dots,” are a specialized class of CDs that have been modified through supramolecular chemistry techniques (Patent application no. PCT/IB2023/056725). Supra-CDs are designed to address some of the challenges associated with traditional CDs when used as additives in materials like concrete. Supra-CDs are typically functionalized with molecular entities that create non-covalent interactions between CDs and other components in a material (Arcudi and Đorđević, 2023). These non-covalent interactions offer several benefits, such as enhancing the dispersion of supra-CDs within the material matrix. This enhanced dispersion helps in achieving a more uniform distribution of CDs throughout the material, reducing the risk of agglomeration, and ensuring that the CDs can contribute more effectively to the material's properties. The non-covalent functionalization of supra-CDs can make them more compatible with the surrounding matrix, such as cement in the case of concrete. This improved compatibility reduces the likelihood of debonding or detaching CDs during the material's lifecycle, increasing their effectiveness.While the specific mechanisms by which supra-CDs achieve these benefits may vary depending on the supramolecular chemistry techniques employed, their use holds significant potential for overcoming the challenges associated with traditional CDs in concrete and other materials. Nevertheless, there are few literatures on its application in the concrete field (Qu, 2022; Song et al., 2023; Long et al., 2023).

From our literature review, the study regarding CDs in concrete is lacking. In this study, we present a novel utilization of supra-GQDs in cement-based composites and provide insights into the underlying mechanisms that influence compressive strength, flexural strength, micropores, phase formation, and scanning electron micrograph (SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). This research highlights the mechanisms of incorporating the supra-GQDs in cement such that the materials can be utilized as an innovative nanomaterial alternative for enhancing the properties of cement-based materials. These groundbreaking advancements pave the way for an extended exploration of promising solutions for the emergence of novel concrete technology for HSC.

All the experiments used ordinary portland cement (OPC) Type I conforming to ASTM C150 (ASTM C150/C150M-22, 2022) with a specific gravity of 3.13 and a Blaine surface area of 3765 cm2/g. The chemical composition of OPC was obtained through X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysis using a Bruker S8 Tiger apparatus. Fine aggregate was well-graded natural sand conforming to ASTM C33 (ASTM C33-18, 2018) with a specific gravity of 2.56, a fineness modulus of 2.67, and water absorption of 0.65 %. The properties of OPC and supra-GQDs are presented in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. Supra-GQDs assemblies in fine powder form (namely “BC”) were procured from CrystalLyte company, Thailand. The physical and chemical characteristics of supra-GQDs are shown as microstructural measurements. Polycarboxylate-base superplasticizer from Sika, Thailand was procured for improving the workability of fresh mixtures. The superplasticizer, which is a light brownish liquid, does not contain chloride and has a pH of 4.97 and a density of 1.060 g/cm3. To prevent the supra-GQDs for their agglomeration and ensure proper dispersion, the superplasticizer was combined with mixing water. The dosage of the superplasticizer was 1 % of the cement's weight, which helped to adjust the composite cement system's effect on the water-to-cement ratio of the fresh cement mortar.

The material quantities for six mixture proportions were prepared per ASTM C109 (ASTM C109/C109M-16, 2016). The mix proportions contain different dosages of BC as cement additives. A control mortar mix and 1.2 % QDs solution were also prepared for comparison. BC were first mixed in dry mix at 0, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, and 1.2 % by weight of OPC. Our preliminary investigations indicated that concentrations within this range facilitated the optimal dispersion of nanomaterials within the matrix. Lower concentrations may lead to inadequate dispersion, while higher concentrations might result in agglomeration, affecting the overall performance of the nanocomposite. Each mix was assigned a unique name based on the amounts of BC used, such as mix BC-1.2, which contains 1.2 % BC. It is noted that our preliminary study also presented 1.2 % was the optimal dosage in this study. These mixes were prepared in the laboratory for experimental purposes. Fig. 1 illustrate the particle size distribution of fine aggregate. Mortar mix proportions of raw materials used in the experiments are summarized in Table 3 .

OPC composites with and without supra-GQDs were also prepared to gain a better understanding of their microstructural shifts. Fine aggregate was excluded from these pastes, and the other ingredients were prepared similarly to those of mortar mixtures. The preparation procedures were like that of mortar without the addition of fine aggregates. It should be noted there that the different amounts of water used for the mortar mixtures, particularly in the case of BC and QDs, were based on the specific dispersion requirements of the materials. For BC, a superplasticizer was employed to achieve proper dispersion and prevent agglomeration, reducing the overall water demand. In contrast, for QDs, no dispersion process was performed, and hence, a higher amount of water was needed.

The initial step was weighing and batching the constituent materials for each mixture. To achieve proper dispersion of BC particles and prevent agglomeration, the following procedure was implemented: A beaker was used to combine a portion of the mixing water (approximately 500 g) and the superplasticizer. The BC particles were then added to the mixture of water and superplasticizer and manually stirred with a stirring rod until the particles completely dissolved. Afterward, an ultrasonicator operating at 60 Hz (280 W) was utilized to sonicate the suspension for 1 h, resulting in uniform and homogeneous particle suspension (Win et al., 2023; Adamu, 2021). The mixing and curing procedures for the specimens followed the guidelines outlined in ASTM C305 (ASTM C305-20, 2020). OPC and fine aggregate were first accurately measured and added to the mechanical mixer, where they were mixed for approximately 1 min. Subsequently, the remaining mixing water and the particle suspension consisting of water, BC, and superplasticizer were added in sequence and mixed until a homogeneous mixture was achieved. The entire mixing process took around 3 min. Once a homogeneous mortar mix was obtained, the fresh properties of the mortar were assessed. The workability was determined using a flow table test in accordance with the specifications of ASTM C1437 (ASTM C1437-20, 2020). Following the evaluation of the fresh mortar, it was cast into molds and cured in a controlled environment at a temperature of 23 ± 2 ˚C, along with 95 % relative humidity, for 24 h before demolding. After demolding, the samples were promptly transferred to a curing tank filled with lime-saturated water to undergo further curing before undergoing testing (ASTM C511-21, 2021). A triplicate sample was performed for each test.

The overview of material preparation and experimental studies is depicted in Fig. 2 and Table 4 , respectively. The initial setting times of supra-GQDs cement pastes with different dosages were investigated to provide the early-stage hydration (ASTM C191-21, 2021). The studied properties of hardened mortar composites include bulk density (ASTM C642-21, 2021), volume of permeable voids (VPV) (ASTM C642-21, 2021), water absorption (ASTM C642-21, 2021), compressive strength (ASTM C109/C109M-16, 2016), and flexural strength (ASTM C348-21, 2021). The specimens tested for the 28-day bulk density, 28-day VPV, 28-day water absorption, and 3-, 7-, 28-day compressive strengths were 50-mm mortar cubes. For the 7- and 28-day flexural strength test, beam specimens with dimensions of 40 mm × 40 mm × 160 mm were prepared.

The Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) isotherm is a promising method for assessing the adsorption properties of various materials (De Belie et al., 2010). In this study, BET results were employed to characterize the pore properties of cement composites, which are closely linked to their strength. This technique involved subjecting the sample to a vacuum, causing the cement matrix to adsorb nitrogen gas. A nitrogen adsorption measurement instrument (Micromeritics 3Flex, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) was used to quantify the amount of adsorbed nitrogen gas, providing insights into different adsorption capacities. 1 g of the 28-day cured sample was used for pore structure analysis of cement composites. The pore analysis covers sizes from 1 to 500 nm according to the BET theory. The results for the pore size and pore volume of cement composites are reported accordingly.

Additionally, the powder samples from 28-day cured sample pastes were sampled and analyzed using a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument (Perkin Elmer Pyris 1 instrument) for examining their phase formation. The TGA experiment involved heating the samples from 25 to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min while maintaining a continuous flow of nitrogen (70 ml/min).

Moreover, the microstructural morphology of the cement paste composites was examined through the SEM-EDS observation by using SUPRA 66VP SEM instrument. The SEM instrument was used to record and analyze the atomic-scale surface morphology of the specimens. To enhance electrical conductivity, the fracture surface of cement composite pastes underwent the Au-coating process prior to testing. Subsequently, the coated samples were inserted into the SEM machine, allowing the capture of images throughout a span of 6000 revolutions. The observed SEM images are commonly found morphology during the observation. The experimental processes are illustrated in Fig. 3 .

Supra-CDs are the assemblies of CDs through covalent or non-covalent bond, which generates 1D, 2D or 3D architectural structures from zero-dimensional CDs structure resulting in novel properties used in various applications (Arcudi and Đorđević, 2023; Li, 2023; Mokoloko et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022). The formation of supra-CDs is simply achievable by thermal annealing of CDs at 200–900 °C under N2 environment, without any other chemicals (Mokoloko et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022; Mokoloko, 2021; Mokoloko, 2022). By thermal annealing the GQDs solution under N2 environment at 300 °C for 1 h followed by 600 °C for 1 h, there exists the formation of supra-GQDs in the solid form. As revealed by SEM-EDX (Fig. 4 (a)), the formation of 3D sheet-like morphology of as-synthesized supra-GQDs comprising 75.86 % of Carbon, 10.99 % of N, 6.53 % of O, 6.3 % of S by atom suggests the assemblies of GQDs in the 2D GQDs sheet, which is further stacked into the 3D morphology. The presence of (002) plane of graphite at 2q = 25.4° with the broad peak, (See Fig. 4(b)), which is generally attributed to (002) plane of graphite in reduced graphene oxide (Qazi, 2022) accompanying with Raman spectra showing D band at 1359 cm−1 and G band at 1561 cm−1 corresponding to defects in graphitic structure and graphitic carbon, respectively (Xu and Hu, 2012; Korepanov, 2017; Pang, 2010; Yolshina, 2017; Frezzotti, 2019; Afandi et al., 2018) confirms the graphitic nature of as-synthesized 3D sheet-like supra-GQDs, (See Fig. 4(c)). Moreover, TEM images clearly demonstrate the assemblies of GQDs clusters impregnated into 3D sheet-like structure (See Fig. 4(d)), validating GQDs as the building blocks of the 3D sheet-like supra-GQDs. Generally, without a template, GQDs are preferentially transformed into 2D sheet structure (Zhou et al., 2022). π -π interactions among 2D sheet GQDs are suggested to be key factors making the as-synthesized 2D sheet GQDs to stack into 3D morphology since such interactions has also been reported to strongly affect the assemblies of GQDs (Arcudi and Đorđević, 2023). Appearance of supra-GQDs is illustrated in Table 5 . Table 6 presents the impurities and elemental compositions of supra-GQDs obtained using Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Sulfur (CHNOS) method and Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS), conducted using Thermo Flash 2000 CHNS/O analyzers and Thermo Fisher iCAP Qc instruments.

Fig. 5 shows the effect of adding supra-GQDs in cement composites on their initial setting times. Results indicate that the initial setting time of utilizing supra-GQDs is quicker than control sample at ambient temperature. It can clearly be seen that supra-GQDs significantly changes the setting time of the cement. It is inferred that the CDs are supposed to accelerate hydration process of the cement since CDs may offer nucleation sites (Long et al., 2023). At the same time, it can be inferred that supra-GQDs cement pastes possess more C–S–H gels. As is well known, C–S–H gels are able to enhance the mechanical characteristics.

The effects of the dosage of BC on the mechanical properties of hardened cement composites cured for different ages are shown in Fig. 6 (a). Test results indicate that the 3-,7-, and 28-day compressive strengths of concrete with the addition of 0.3 % of supra-GQDs are 77.1, 55.0, and 15.1 % higher than the control, respectively. The supra-GQDs apparently increases the compressive strengths of final products. It is worth noting that the difference between the compressive strength for 3 and 28 curing days for the control one is significantly larger than that of 0.3 % of supra-GQDs, representing that using supra-GQDs in cement offers not only enhanced compressive strength, but also accelerated the hydration process. For instance, when adding 0.3 % of supra-GQDs, the concrete can have a similar 3-day strength value to the 28-day compressive strength of the control.

Fig. 6(b) shows the plot of the increase in % strength enhancement versus the BC’s dosages. Results indicate that the % strength enhancement linearly increases as a function of additive dosages for all different curing times. These results confirm the role of supra-GQDs as the compressive strength enhancer. Furthermore, a small gap between 7- and 28-day compressive strength values was observed for all % dosages of supra-GQDs added. Figs. 6(c) and 5(d) demonstrated a similar trend for the improvement of flexural strength as a function of the dosage of supra-GQDs. The flexural strength of concrete when adding supra-GQDs increases to the greater extents than that of compressive strength. For example, for the addition of 1.2 % of supra-GQDs, the 28-day flexural strength and compressive strengths are 108.3 % and 39.2 %, respectively, when compared to the control. This means better improvement effect in flexural strength. Even though supra-GQDs are 0D, their two-dimensional structure and special surface properties made it easier for them to interact at the nanoscale, which could lead to better flexural strength. From the mechanical properties, the optimal dosage of supra-GQDs added into the concrete is 1.2 %. The reason for the improvement in the mechanical properties from adding supra-GQDs can be revealed by the physical properties and microstructural analysis of the concrete.

The microstructural analysis of paste with 1.2 % addition of supra-GQDs was observed using SEM-EDX analysis and shown in Fig. 7 (a). Observations indicate that there exists a significant number of areas where needle-like C-S-H phases grow on, which was not observed in the control case. The specific characteristics of GQDs act as nucleation sites for the preferential growth of needle-like C-S-H structures (Long et al., 2023). These needle-like C-S-H phases seem to have a compact structure with pattern alignments and joint other phases together, thus reducing void in the concrete structure. This void is the key component deteriorating the mechanical properties and durability in the concrete (Qu, 2022). Moreover, there are no obvious supra-GQDs clusters shown in the SEM-EDX micrographs and the needle-like C-S-H clusters scattered around the matrix; meaningly, there is a dissociation of supra-GQDs into small GQDs clusters during the early hydration process. Besides, these small GQDs clusters can act as nucleation sites for formation of needle-like C-S-H phases. The nucleation of C-S-H by CDs or GO, which is the extended form of GQDs, has been reported in the past literatures (Qu, 2022; Basquiroto de Souza et al., 2022; Balaji and Swathika, 2022; Wang and Zhong, 2023).

These SEM results are consistent with the VPV results measured, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Results revealed that the VPV values were almost reduced in half when 0.3 % supra-GQDs added in the concrete. Furthermore, they gradually decreased with increasing supra-GQDs addition, suggesting the role of supra-GQDs to be nucleation site for needle-like C-S-H responsible for filling up the void. The 28-day water absorption as shown in Fig. 7(c), results are also consistent with the VPV results. The addition of supra-GQDs can reduce water absorption values of hardened composites by up to 45 % when compared to the control. As aforementioned, the lower the VPV values, the better the concrete is. This water is hard to penetrate in the cement matrix resulting in enhanced durability.

For the 28-day density, the results of different dosages of supra-GQDs of composites, compared to the control, are shown in Fig. 7(d). In agreement with other characterized results, the density of the concrete with supra-GQDs increased according to less void and more compact structure as well, compared to the control one, especially for 1.2 % in which the density rises almost 5 %.

The 28-day compressive strength, 28-day flexural strength, and 28-day VPV of the concrete containing 1.2 % supra-GQDs and 1.2 % QDs solution are illustrated in Fig. 8 (a)–(c). Results indicate the use of GQDs in the supra-GQDs form significantly enhances the compressive and flexural strengths for the concretes. Their compressive and flexural strengths of the supra-GQDs are approximately 27.9 and 57.9 % higher than the QDs solution, respectively. For the VPV results, it is found that the supra-GQDs mixture has VPV values lower than the QDs solution by approximately 39 %. This implies that the permeable voids of concrete having supra-GQDs are significantly less than the concrete having QDs solution. The permeable voids can finally affect the durability where the denser concrete will perform better.

For the pore volume in microscales, the results of the supra-GQDs and QDs solution are presented in Fig. 8(d). Results indicate that the supra-GQDs has lower dV/dw pore volume than QDs solution at any sizes ranging from 1.7 to 211 nm. This implies that adding supra-GQDs in cement can lead to reduced nano-, meso-, and micro-pores, and these benefits the denser cement matrices in any sizes as compared with the GQDs in general liquid form. The results of total pore volume also revealed that the BC-1.2 in cement leads to 30 % reduction when compared to the QDs-1.2. The incorporation of supra-GQDs effectively generates the development of cement composites with finer pore structures. The results suggest that introducing supra-GQDs into cement-based materials brings about improvements in the structure of pores, reduces overall porosity, and enhances the compactness of the material. Consequently, these alterations contribute to the observed enhancement in mechanical properties. Additionally, the incorporation of supra-GQDs appears to positively impact both the microstructure and macroscopic characteristics of the cement-based composite, leading to increased strength and durability.

Due to the high influence of the thermal energy towards the decomposition of GQDs (Liu, 2021), GQDs in general form are likely to decompose into gaseous products upon initiating the highly exothermic hydration reaction, thus worsening the strength enhancement performance. However, with the supra-GQDs structure, the decomposition temperature is by far improved as revealed by TGA results under N2 environment (Fig. 8(e)) demonstrating that % weight loss was less than 30 % even at 1000 °C, thus preventing the GQDs structure from local heat from hydration. Upon hydration reaction proceeds, in case of supra-GQDs, the local heat would be used to break the covalent and non-covalent bond among GQDs clusters in the supra-GQDs, instead of decomposing the GQDs into gaseous product in case of GQDs in the general form. The thermal stability and decomposition characteristics of nanomaterials play a crucial role in controlling their behavior during the cement hydration process, consequently influencing the mechanical properties of the resulting nanocomposites. By avoiding excessive decomposition and gaseous product release, supra-GQDs promote a more controlled and beneficial interaction with the cementitious matrix. The utilization of local heat to break bonds enhances the structural integrity, strength, and durability of the mortar nanocomposites. This understanding of the thermal behavior of GQDs and supra-GQDs informs the rationale behind their differential effects on the mechanical performance of the resulting cementitious materials. Moreover, TGA curve displays that there is no difference between supra-GQDs and GQDs solution. This infers the chemical composition of the final cement products are similar. Adding either supra-GQDs or GQDs solution does not change the phase formation from cement hydration reactions.

It should be noted that although the decomposition temperature for supra-GQDs reaction is very high, the detachment among GQDs clusters requires much less energy as revealed by Fig. 8(f), which demonstrates that supra-GQDs can even be dissociated into GQDs clusters under low temperature and low oxidation conditions. These results are consistent with the SEM-EDX results discussed earlier, showing that there was no observation of supra-GQDs in concrete, which implies that supra-GQDs are detached under concrete formation. In the hydration process, the local heat from the exothermic reaction would be preferred to break the bond among GQDs clusters to a large extent, so the concept seems feasible. For overall mechanism (Fig. 8(g)), unlike GQDs in general form that would be partly decomposed when hydration reaction occurs, in the case of supra-GQDs, upon the hydration reaction is initiated, GQDs clusters in the supra-GQDs will be detached. These GQDs clusters are then the nucleation site for the formation of C-S-H phase in the mortar, thus improving the mechanical properties of the mortar. Nanomaterials, including graphene derivatives, have been shown to facilitate cement hydration through nucleation effect, wherein the surface of graphene nanomaterials adsorbs calcium ions, promoting the crystallization of hydrates and accelerating the hydration process. Additionally, graphene nanomaterials can tightly bond to C-S-H bundles, acting as bridges and enhancing adhesive performance. This synergy between the bridge and nucleation effects results in the formation of high-density, high-stiffness C-S-H gels with a denser microstructure (Meng, 2021; Long, 2022).
Although this research has been focused on chemical interactions and early-age properties, some limitations of this works can be included such as:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Limited Exposure Time and Scale: The study primarily focuses on short-term performance assessments (3, 7, and 28 days) of concrete samples containing supra-GQDs. While these initial results are promising, a more comprehensive understanding of the long-term behavior, especially in large-scale applications and under varied environmental conditions, is essential.

Specific Application Focus: The investigation is centered around the application of supra-GQDs in thermal conductive pavements and precast concretes. Extending the scope to various construction contexts and applications would provide a more holistic view of supra-GQDs' versatility and effectiveness in different scenarios.

Environmental Impact Considerations: Although the environmental impact of supra-GQDs-based concrete is suggested for future research, our study does not provide a detailed analysis of the environmental sustainability of supra-GQDs using a life cycle assessment method. Further research is needed to assess the overall ecological footprint and sustainability of using supra-GQDs as a green construction material.

ce:section.ce:para:
This study investigated the effects of incorporating supra-GQDs in green cement composites, examining mechanical properties, BET isotherm analysis, TGA, and microstructure. The key findings were summarized as follows:
1)
The utilization of supra-GQDs resulted in a significant enhancement in compressive and flexural strengths compared to GQDs solutions, demonstrating a concentration-dependent relationship. This improvement can be attributed to the exceptional physical strength of supra-GQDs and their lower functional group content, fostering robust bonds with cement hydration products.
2)
Significant properties improvements were observed in supra-GQDs cement composites, including increased VPV, reduced water absorption, and increased hardened density.
3)
BET testing revealed that supra-GQDs addition refined the pore structure, resulting in a matrix characterized by reduced nano-, meso-, and micro-pore sizes and a decrease in total pore volume.
4)
TGA results indicated a significant impact of supra-GQDs on the main hydrates of cement, signifying an increase in the degree of hydration reactions.
5)
SEM micrographs demonstrated that the inclusion of supra-GQDs in the cement paste led to a denser C-S-H phase, with apparent nano-bridging mechanisms observed in the C-S-H structure.

While our study provides valuable insights, it is essential to acknowledge certain limitations. The lack of long-term performance data for large-scale concrete samples and specific environmental conditions limits the comprehensive understanding of supra-GQDs' behavior over time.

To build on the current study, future research should consider:
1)
Long-term performance assessments: Investigate the durability and performance of concrete samples containing supra-GQDs over extended periods, considering different environmental conditions.
2)
Diverse applications: Explore additional applications beyond thermal conductive pavements and precast concretes to understand the versatility of supra-GQDs in various construction contexts.
3)
Environmental impact: Assess the environmental sustainability of supra-GQDs-based concrete to ensure its viability as a green construction material.

In conclusion, while our findings present a promising solution for green HSC using supra-GQDs, acknowledging research limitations and suggesting avenues for future exploration are crucial for advancing the field. Ongoing research is aimed at investigating large-scale concrete samples, subjecting them to tests to assess their suitability as thermal conductive materials for specific applications, such as thermal conductive pavements and precast concretes for electric vehicles.

Thwe Thwe Win: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lapyote Prasittisopin: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Rungkiat Nganglumpoon: Investigation. Piriya Pinthong: Investigation. Suthasinee Watmanee: Investigation. Weerachon Tolek: Investigation. Joongjai Panpranot: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to the Second Century Fund (C2F) for the postdoctoral researcher fellowship at Chulalongkorn University. This project was also funded by National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and Chulalongkorn University (N42A660629), and by Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fund Chulalongkorn University (SOCF67250015). The authors thank CrystalLyte Co., Ltd., for providing the graphene quantum dots. The authors also thank the Centre of Excellence in Innovative Construction Materials of Chulalongkorn University for the lab equipment.
